steps.m: to glorify, to praise, to declare perfection

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding kasra at the end makes it a command
Root means 'high/elevated' and became 'name' - a word that lifts up and identifies something
Changed to show this is what's being glorified in the command 'glorify the name'. The ٱ is a different way of writing اِ, and the َ shows it's being acted upon.
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun for someone who does this action completely, showing complete nurturing and care
Adding kasra (ِ) shows this word is possessed by something after it
Adding كَ at the end means 'your' - it shows the Lord belongs to you (when speaking to one person)
These three letters together represent the basic concept of being high or elevated
Added أ at start and vowel marks to make it mean 'the most' or 'the highest degree' of being elevated. This form is used to show something has the maximum amount of a quality.

steps.m: The Most High (a name/attribute of رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it definite and in this context it becomes one of Allah's names/attributes, describing His supreme elevation above all creation
This is the basic form of the word that points to someone or something we're about to describe. It's like saying 'who' or 'which' in English.

steps.m: The one who (connects to خَلَقَ - created)
steps.n: Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it more specific. This word connects to خَلَقَ (created) in the next part, pointing to Allah who did the creating.

steps.m: to create, make, or form something from nothing
he created (followed by فَسَوَّىٰ - and then perfected)
steps.n: Added fathas (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to what comes after it (فَسَوَّىٰ) showing two actions: first He created, then He perfected.
Connected the root letters and added shadda on و to show intensive meaning of making something completely perfect or proportioned
Added فَ at the beginning to show this action follows after the previous one (after He created)
Added ى at the end to show the word is complete
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'that' in English
Adding ال makes it more specific and adds emphasis. The ّ (shadda) shows the ل is doubled for stronger pronunciation

steps.m: And the one who/that which (subject of قَدَّرَ)
steps.n: The و connects this word to what came before it. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَدَّرَ (determined/measured) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled د (shown by ّ) makes the meaning more intense and precise. This word is connected to فَهَدَىٰ (and then He guided) which comes after it.
Added fathas to connect letters. The ي at the end becomes ى (alif maqsurah) because it's at the end of the word.

steps.m: and thus he guided (after قَدَّرَ - he determined)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after Allah determined things. Also added the small alif (ٰ) to help with pronunciation.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence, like saying 'who' or 'which' in English.
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: and the one who/which (subject of أَخْرَجَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects it to what came before. The word is the subject (doer) of the verb أَخْرَجَ (brought forth) in this ayah.

steps.m: and the one who/which (subject of أَخْرَجَ)
steps.n: Added a long vowel mark (ٓ) at the end which helps with pronunciation when continuing to the next word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes it mean 'caused something to exit/come out'. In this ayah, it's talking about Allah bringing forth the pasture (المرعى) from the earth.
This is the basic meaning of the root letters
Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'the act of grazing' by adding vowel marks
Added مَ at the beginning to show where the action happens. The ى at the end replaces ي because it's at the end of the word.
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific. The small alif (ٰ) at the end shows it's being pronounced longer.

steps.m: to make something into something else

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past

steps.m: so he made (something into something else)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happened as a result of what came before in the previous ayah

steps.m: so he made it (the pasture into غُثَآءً أَحْوَىٰ/dark debris)
steps.n: Adds هُ meaning 'it' to show what is being transformed. In this ayah, it refers to the pasture that Allah transformed into dark debris.

steps.m: to become dried up and scattered

steps.n: Changed the word from an action to a thing by adding the 'u' sound at the start and making the middle letter silent
Changed the end to make it a noun describing the result of something drying up and breaking apart

steps.m: dried up, scattered plant matter (object of فَجَعَلَهُ/made it)
steps.n: Added ً at the end to show this is what was made into something (the object of فَجَعَلَهُ/made it)
Added short vowels (fatha-kasra-fatha) to make it mean 'it became dark'
Added أ at the beginning to make it comparative, showing something is more dark/black than something else
Added the elongated alif (ٰ) at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning. This word describes how Allah made the dry grass (غُثَآءً) dark colored.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added أ at beginning to show making someone else do the action
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by adding 'nu' at start and changing vowels
Added س at beginning to show this will happen in the future

steps.m: we will make you recite (فَلَا تَنسَىٰٓ)
steps.n: Added ك at end to show you are the one who will be made to recite. This connects to the next phrase فَلَا تَنسَىٰٓ (and you will not forget)
Basic negation particle that means 'not'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this negation follows as a result of what came before. This word negates the verb تَنسَىٰٓ (forget) that comes after it.

steps.m: to forget or leave something behind

steps.n: Basic past tense verb showing someone forgot something

steps.m: you forget (after فَلَا meaning 'so you will not')
steps.n: Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' as the doer. The ي at the end changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end. Added sukun on ن to show it's present tense.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to help with pronunciation
This is the base form of the exception particle that introduces exceptions in sentences. In this ayah, it introduces an exception to what Allah wills.
Added shadda and alif to strengthen and complete the exception meaning. This connects to مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ (what Allah wills) which follows it in the ayah.

steps.m: what (connects to شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - Allah wills)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that links to what comes after it. In this ayah, it connects 'except' (إِلَّا) with 'Allah wills' (شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ), meaning 'except what Allah wills'
Added the vowels 'a' (fatha) to each letter to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
The middle letter ي becomes ا because it's a weak letter, making the word easier to pronounce
The long alif (آ) makes the word stronger and more emphatic
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's the subject
This particle adds emphasis to a sentence
Added هُۥ (he) to show who the statement is about. This word connects to the next verb يَعْلَمُ (he knows) by telling us who is doing the knowing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Added يَ at the start to show it's happening now, added sukun on ل to make it easier to say, and added ُ at the end to show 'he' is doing the action
Changes the action into a noun meaning the actual act of making something public. This word is what يَعْلَمُ (he knows) is acting on - he knows the open/public things.

steps.m: making something public/open (object of يَعْلَمُ - he knows)
steps.n: Adds a small 'a' sound at the end to show this word is being acted upon by 'he knows'

steps.m: the public/open things (object of يَعْلَمُ - he knows)
steps.n: Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article
This is the base word meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it refers to what is hidden, connecting to يَخْفَىٰ (is hidden).
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense. The final ي of the root becomes ى because it's at the end of the word. This word is something that Allah knows, as mentioned in يَعْلَمُ (He knows).
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) which is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning
Changes to an intransitive verb showing the quality of being easy
The shadda makes the meaning causative - showing someone is making something else easy
Changed to present tense 'we' form by adding نُ at start and changing final vowel
Added ك to show who receives the action (you)

steps.m: and we make easy for you (connected to لِلْيُسْرَىٰ)
steps.n: Added 'and' (و) at the start to connect this with the next phrase about making things easy
Changed from root letters to a simple noun by adding vowel marks, making it mean 'ease'
Added ى at the end to create feminine form indicating 'the easier way'
Added ال to make it definite ('the easier way')

steps.m: for the easier way (connected to وَنُيَسِّرُكَ - and We will ease you)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show purpose/direction. This word connects to the previous verb وَنُيَسِّرُكَ (and We will ease you), showing what you are being eased towards.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added shadda and kasra to make it intensive, showing strong emphasis on the action
Added sukun at the end to make it a command
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence (connects to previous ayah)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects with the verb نَّفَعَتِ (it benefits) to show 'if it benefits'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter
Added ت and ِ at the end to show this is addressing a feminine 'you'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun meaning the act or result of remembering
Adds ى at the end to make a special type of reminder (like a warning or lesson)

steps.m: the reminder (used in this ayah to refer to the Quran's reminders)
steps.n: Adds 'the' (ال) at the start and doubles the ذ sound - like changing 'reminder' to 'the reminder'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Doubles the ذ letter to show strong/intense remembering

steps.m: he will remember deeply/take heed (referring to مَن يَخْشَىٰ - whoever fears)
steps.n: Added سَ at the start to show this will happen in the future. This word refers to 'whoever fears' in the next part of the ayah.
This is a special word that connects parts of a sentence by referring to people. Here it refers to the person who 'fears' (يَخْشَىٰ).
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation

steps.m: to be in awe/fear of something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb

steps.m: he fears/is in awe (subject of سَيَذَّكَّرُ)
steps.n: Added يَ at start to show present tense. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a present tense verb. This word describes who will remember in سَيَذَّكَّرُ (will remember).

steps.m: he fears/is in awe (subject of سَيَذَّكَّرُ)
steps.n: Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's a long vowel sound. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to avoid, to stay away from the side

steps.n: Adding ت at start and doubling ن shows doing the action purposely to oneself

steps.m: he deliberately avoids (something) (connected to الْأَشْقَى - the most wretched)
steps.n: The ي at start shows a male is doing this action now or in future. This person is described as الْأَشْقَى (the most wretched) in the ayah.

steps.m: he deliberately avoids it (referring to نَارًا - the Fire mentioned earlier)
steps.n: The ها at the end refers back to نَارًا (the Fire) mentioned in a previous ayah

steps.m: and he deliberately avoids it (the Fire)
steps.n: The وَ (and) at start connects this to previous statements about the most wretched person
Added أ at start and ى at end to make it mean 'most' (like saying 'most miserable' instead of just 'miserable')

steps.m: the most wretched/miserable one (who will avoid the reminder)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to specify we're talking about a particular most wretched person mentioned in this context
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that points to someone or something

steps.m: the one who (will enter النَّارَ the Fire)
steps.n: Adding ٱلَّ at the beginning makes it point to something specific. In this verse, it points to the person who will enter the great Fire.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The ي at the end becomes ى because it's at the end of a verb. This verb describes what الَّذِى (the one who) does.
The root letters form a noun meaning 'fire'. The middle letter و changes to ا, which is a common pattern for this type of word.
The shadda emphasizes the intensity of the fire
Added ٱل to show this is a specific fire

steps.m: the intense fire (object of يَصْلَى/he burns in)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this fire is what is being burned in (object of the verb يَصْلَى)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels and added ى at the end to make it mean 'the most great' in feminine form

steps.m: the greatest (referring to النَّارَ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and an alif to extend the final sound. This word describes how great/intense the Fire is.
This is the basic form of the word that connects events in sequence, like saying 'and after that' in English
Adding the damma (ُ) makes the word stronger and more definite

steps.m: then, next, afterwards (shows sequence of events)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) makes the word even stronger, showing a clear break or pause between events. In this ayah, it connects to what happens next in the sequence.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَمُوتُ (he dies) mean 'he does not die'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he dies (in this ayah: will die)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show present/future tense. The middle و takes a damma (ُ) because it's a hollow verb. The final ُ shows this is happening to 'he'.
This is a preposition that shows location

Added ها at the end which means 'it' and refers back to Hell (جَهَنَّمَ) mentioned earlier in the surah. This word is part of describing what happens to the disbelievers in Hell - they will neither die in it nor live.
Added fatha to connect this word to what follows
Added the negation particle لَا which negates the verb يَحْيَىٰ (lives) that follows it in the ayah
The root letters show the basic idea of living
Added يَ at beginning to show 'he' does the action in present tense. The last ي changed to ا because it's at the end of the word.
Changed the final ا to ى with an alif above (ٰ) which is just a different way of writing the same sound
This is a common particle that comes before verbs to emphasize that something has definitely happened
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word comes before أَفْلَحَ (he has succeeded) to emphasize that the success has definitely happened.

steps.m: to succeed, thrive, achieve what is desired
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of success or prosperity
Adding fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone succeed'
This is a word that refers to people and connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the verb أَفْلَحَ (has succeeded) to show who succeeded.
Added basic past tense vowels. The و changes to ا at the end of past tense verbs.
The shadda and ta- prefix show that the person is doing the action to themselves
The word has an alif (ى) added at the end to show it's the end of the word, but the meaning stays the same
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense. This word is connected to فَصَلَّىٰ (he prayed) in this ayah - first he remembered, then he prayed.
Added 'and' at the beginning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from 'being high' to 'name' by changing vowels and adding nunation (double dots) at end
Added ٱ prefix and changed final vowel to fatha because it's the object of the verb ذَكَرَ (he mentioned/remembered)

steps.m: to care for and help grow
one who cares for and helps others grow completely
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this person does a lot of caring and helping

steps.m: the one who cares for (possessed by اسم)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this word means 'my Lord' - like saying 'the Lord of me'
Added هِۦ to show this is his Lord

steps.m: to pray (literally: to connect with Allah)

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding the vowel marks 'fatha' (َ) to show 'he prayed'
The doubled letter makes the action more intense or emphatic
The alif at the end shows this is a past tense verb
Added فَ to show this action follows what came before in the verse (mentioning Allah's name)

steps.m: but rather (contradicting the previous statement and introducing 'you prefer the worldly life')
steps.n: This word shows that what comes next (you prefer the worldly life) is different from or contradicts what was said before
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding تُ prefix

steps.m: you all prefer (addressing the disbelievers in بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people are being addressed. This verb is speaking to the disbelievers who prefer worldly life.
This root is about life and living
Added vowel marks (fatha) between the root letters to make the basic verb 'to live'
Added و and ة to make it into a noun meaning 'life'

steps.m: the life (object of تُؤْثِرُونَ - you prefer)
steps.n: Added ال to mean 'the' and َ at the end because it's the object of the verb 'you prefer' in this ayah

steps.m: to be near or close to something
steps.n: This root is about nearness or closeness
Changed the root letters to make 'dunya' - the closest/nearest world to us (feminine form)

Added ال to make it specific. The shadda (ّ) appears on the د because it comes after ال. This word is describing 'life' (ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ) in this ayah, referring to life in this world as opposed to the afterlife.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense verb
Changed first two letters to a long alif (آ) and added a kasra (i) sound to make it mean 'later/last' instead of 'came after'

steps.m: the life to come, next life
steps.n: Adding feminine ending ة changes meaning to refer specifically to the next life

steps.m: and the life to come (subject of خَيْرٌ)
steps.n: Added 'wa' (and) at start + 'al' (the) to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: and the life to come (subject of خَيْرٌ)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is doing the action, and merged وَالْ into ٱلْ
Changes the word into a basic noun pattern
Added the ٌ ending to show this is the main quality being described about الآخِرَةُ (the Hereafter).

steps.m: to stay, to last, to remain

steps.n: Changed to comparative form (like 'more' or '-er' in English)
Added 'and' to connect with خَيْرٌ (better) in the previous phrase
Added vertical fatḥa mark which is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change meaning
This is a basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger - like adding an exclamation mark in English. Here it emphasizes that 'this' (هٰذَا) is definitely true.
The shadda (ّ) makes the ن sound stronger to show extra emphasis
This word starts with ها which gets people's attention before pointing to something
Adding ذا makes it point to something specific that's nearby

steps.m: this (points to something nearby) (subject of لَفِى)
steps.n: Added vowel marks and alif to help with pronunciation. This word is the subject that لَفِى (indeed in) is talking about in this ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'in' that connects this word to ٱلصُّحُفِ (the scriptures)
The لَ prefix adds emphasis because this word follows إِنَّ (indeed/verily). Together إِنَّ and لَ create strong emphasis.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes vowels to make many pages/scrolls instead of just one - like going from 'book' to 'books'
Adds 'the' (ال) to the word, which makes the ص double in sound

steps.m: to be first or come before
steps.n: The root letters represent the basic concept of being first or preceding something else
The damma vowel mark changes it to passive form
Added ى at the end to make it mean 'first' for feminine things (like 'first girl')
Added the definite article ال to specify 'the first' rather than just any 'first'
Changed the vowels to make it plural - this is a special pattern in Arabic for making groups of things

steps.m: of the written pages/scrolls (belonging to إِبْرٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows these scrolls belong to Ibrahim and Musa, like saying 'Ibrahim's scrolls' in English

steps.m: Abraham (the proper name, in the scrolls of إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: This is a proper noun of foreign origin (Hebrew), so it doesn't have an Arabic root. It's the name of the prophet Abraham. In this ayah, it shows whose scrolls are being referred to (the scrolls of Abraham).

steps.m: Abraham (the proper name, in the scrolls of إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: The َ (fatha) at the end shows this name is being acted upon by the word before it (صُحُفِ meaning 'scrolls'). It's like saying 'scrolls of Abraham' in English.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet mentioned in the scrolls of إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: This is a proper noun of foreign origin (Hebrew/Egyptian) referring to the prophet Moses. In this ayah, his scrolls are being mentioned along with those of Ibrahim
The prefix وَ means 'and', connecting this word to what came before it
